title: Tracing a Full Loop Turn
concept: Walking through a single cycle of observe-reason-act and detailing state changes at each step.

exercises.prompt: Quick recap: What is an AI agent, in simple terms?

options.text: A system that looks at a situation and takes actions to reach a goal
A single fixed answer typed by a human
A picture-drawing tool with no goals

exercises.explain: An AI agent senses its situation, thinks about what to do, and acts toward a goal. That sense-think-act pattern is exactly the loop we'll trace step by step in this lesson.

cards.title: Follow One Turn
cards.body: Imagine slowing down an agent to watch just ONE turn. First it observes, then it reasons, then it acts. Today we trace that single cycle and watch what changes.

cards.title: The Observe-Reason-Act Loop
cards.body: A loop turn is one full cycle: the agent observes new information, reasons about it, and acts. Each step updates the agent's 'state', which is its memory of where things stand.

cards.title: Cooking One Step
cards.body: It's like a cook checking the pan (observe), deciding it needs salt (reason), then adding salt (act). After that step, the dish is different, so the next check starts from the new state.

cards.title: A Thermostat Agent
cards.body: Observe: room is 18°C, goal is 21°C. Reason: too cold, heat needed. Act: turn on heater. State change: heater now ON, and the next turn will observe a warmer room.

cards.title: Not All At Once
cards.body: Myth: the agent does everything in one instant blur. Truth: it moves in clear steps, and each step changes the state before the next step begins.

cards.title: Before vs After a Turn
cards.body: One turn always leaves the state different from how it started.

left.text: Room 18°C, heater OFF, goal not met

right.text: Heater ON, room warming, goal closer

exercises.prompt: In a single loop turn, what is the correct order of steps?
exercises.explain: The agent first observes to gather info, then reasons about what it means, then acts on that decision. Acting or reasoning before observing would mean deciding with no information.

exercises.prompt: True or False: After the 'act' step, the agent's state usually changes.
exercises.explain: Acting affects the world or the agent's memory, so the state after acting is different from before. That new state is what the next turn observes.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing step of the loop.
exercises.sentence: The agent will observe the room, reason about it, and then act on its decision.
exercises.explain: Between observing and acting, the agent must reason, deciding what the observation means before choosing an action.

exercises.prompt: Which step-by-step trace looks AI-generated (clean, complete, and follows the loop exactly)?

options.text: Observe: battery at 20%. Reason: low power, need to recharge. Act: move to charger. State: now heading to charger, battery still 20%.
the robot just kinda went to the charger idk why
battery low ok charger done bye
Robot. Charger. Maybe. Something happened somewhere.

exercises.explain: The AI-generated trace is structured and complete: it labels observe, reason, and act clearly and notes the state change. The others are vague, unlabeled, or fragmented.

exercises.prompt: A cleaning robot's goal is a spotless floor. It observes a dirty spot ahead. Which full turn correctly walks through the loop AND shows the state change?

options.text: Observe: dirty spot ahead. Reason: floor not clean yet, should vacuum here. Act: vacuum the spot. State change: that spot now clean, robot moves to check the next area.
Act: vacuum randomly. Observe: nothing. Reason: skip it. State change: none, because it never looked first.
Observe: dirty spot. Act: vacuum. It never reasons and never updates its state.
Reason: floor is clean. Act: shut down. Observe: too late now. State change: unknown.

exercises.explain: A full loop turn goes observe → reason → act, and ends with a real state change. Option A observes the dirty spot, reasons that the goal isn't met, acts by vacuuming, and updates the state (spot clean, moving on). The others skip a step, act before observing, or reason from a wrong assumption, so the loop breaks down.
